title: How Did People Find Out About the Declaration of Independence in 1776?
summary:
Imagine finding out your country just declared independence — but there's no internet, no TV, no phones! That's what happened in 1776. A new book called 'When the Declaration of Independence Was News' tells how people learned about it.

First, printer John Dunlap made posters called broadsides. They were big sheets with the Declaration text. These were sent to state assemblies, military officers, and local leaders. Newspapers copied the text, and people gathered at courthouses and churches for public readings. Some people cheered and tore down symbols of the British king. But others were worried or unhappy.

The famous signed parchment we see today at the National Archives wasn't ready until August. By then, most people already knew! In January 1777, printer Mary Katharine Goddard made the first copies with all the signers' names. She was the only woman printing under her own name at that time.

Historian Emily Sneff wrote the book. She says the Declaration was 'malleable' (easy to change) back then. Different versions had different spellings and formats. Some copies even had handwritten notes. About 125 broadsides from July 1776 survive today.
why_it_matters: This story helps you understand how hard it was to share news before technology, and how important it is to get news quickly and accurately.

keywords.explanation: The document that said the American colonies were free from Britain
Big posters with the Declaration text printed on them
The printer who made the first copies of the Declaration
A woman printer who made copies with all signers' names
When people gathered at courthouses or churches to hear the Declaration read aloud
Feeling unhappy or scared about what independence might mean

questions.question: How did people first find out about the Declaration of Independence in 1776?
Who made the first copies of the Declaration?
Where did people gather to hear the Declaration read aloud?
What did Mary Katharine Goddard do?
options: She printed the first copies with all signers' names
She read the Declaration to crowds
questions.correct_answer: She printed the first copies with all signers' names

questions.question: How did some people react when they heard the Declaration?
options: They cheered and tore down symbols of the king
They wrote letters to the king
questions.correct_answer: They cheered and tore down symbols of the king

questions.question: What does the historian mean by saying the Declaration was 'malleable'?

background_read: Before the internet, TV, or phones, news traveled slowly by horseback, ship, and printed paper.
Paul Revere is famous for riding his horse to warn people about British soldiers in 1775.
The printing press, invented in the 1400s, made it possible to quickly make many copies of documents.
Article_Structure: WHO: Printer John Dunlap, printer Mary Katharine Goddard, and historian Emily Sneff
WHAT: How people learned about the Declaration of Independence in 1776 through broadsides, newspapers, and public readings
WHERE: In the American colonies, especially at courthouses, churches, and in newspapers across the Thirteen Colonies
WHY: Because the Declaration was breaking news, and people needed to know their country had declared independence

perspectives.description: Excited and proud to hear the Declaration read aloud, cheering and tearing down symbols of the king.
Worried or unhappy about independence, fearing what it might mean for their lives and safety.
Interested in how news spread and how different versions of the Declaration were created and shared.
